skill.name: playwright-testing
skill.description: Playwright testing guidance for end-to-end tests, selectors, page objects, fixtures, network mocking, accessibility checks, and debugging workflows.
skill.summary: Helps create, review, and debug reliable Playwright end-to-end tests.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical risk from combined command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Review found no malicious intent or prompt injection; most hits are documentation examples, but the skill includes a local shell setup checker and examples that persist browser auth state, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Setup Checker Executes Project Commands
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for command execution risk in a legitimate diagnostic script. The script runs local shell commands, inspects project files, and calls the Playwright CLI through npx; this is useful for setup validation but can execute local project binaries in an untrusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script and npx call are directly visible, and the behavior is expected for a setup checker. Confidence is below very high because there is no evidence of malicious arguments or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Browser Authentication State May Persist Sensitive Session Data
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for sensitive-data handling risk in documentation. The skill recommends saving Playwright storage state containing cookies and localStorage to a file, which can expose session data if users commit or share the generated file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly describe persisted cookies and localStorage. The pattern is legitimate for Playwright tests, but it still creates a real credential-handling concern.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for most external command hits in markdown. The flagged examples are fenced code blocks or inline command references for normal Playwright use, not hidden execution performed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are markdown examples and review notes rather than executable skill code. The static pattern is noisy because backticks and shell snippets are common in technical documentation.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Patterns Are Local Test Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration risk. The network examples mock application requests or use localhost, and process.env.CI is used only as a common Playwright configuration flag.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples do not send secrets to third-party endpoints and only reference a CI flag or localhost. No evidence of data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Flags Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged review and skill lines discuss Playwright features, test configuration, browser projects, and content recommendations; no weak cryptographic implementation or hostile system reconnaissance was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas found ordinary documentation text and Playwright terminology. The scanner likely matched substrings without confirming crypto or reconnaissance behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Use of Bash and npx in Setup Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup checker uses Bash and invokes npx to verify Playwright availability. This should be treated as a user-approved local diagnostic action, not an automatic background operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern is explicit and relevant to the script. The risk is constrained because there is no evidence of obfuscation, remote download, or hardcoded hostile endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persisted Playwright Storage State
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows saving browser storage state to a file for test authentication. Users should treat this file as sensitive because it can contain cookies or localStorage values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage-state behavior is directly documented and can include session material. The example is legitimate, so the appropriate response is warning rather than blocking.

content.value_statement: End-to-end tests often fail because selectors, waits, and fixtures are fragile. This skill guides Playwright setup, test design, debugging, and maintainable automation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains Playwright configuration for projects, browsers, reporters, traces, screenshots, and web server setup.
Recommends resilient locators using roles, labels, text, chaining, and test IDs as a fallback.
Provides page object and fixture patterns for organizing reusable test behavior.
Shows authentication state reuse, network mocking, API error handling, and slow response simulation.
Covers debugging with Playwright UI mode, traces, screenshots, and version compatibility checks.
Includes accessibility and visual regression testing examples for broader test coverage.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, but it does not guarantee that generated tests match every application state.
The setup checker runs local shell commands and should be used only in trusted project directories.
Authentication state examples can store cookies and local storage, so users must protect generated files.
Some examples require compatible Playwright and package versions before they run unchanged.

use_cases.title: Create New E2E Coverage
use_cases.description: Plan Playwright tests for key user flows with stable locators, clear assertions, and reusable setup.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Flaky Browser Tests
use_cases.description: Replace brittle waits and selectors with Playwright auto-waiting, trace debugging, and targeted assertions.

use_cases.title: Improve Test Architecture
use_cases.description: Introduce page objects, fixtures, authentication state reuse, network mocks, and cross-browser configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Playwright Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single user flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a Playwright test for this user flow. Use role-based locators and explain the main assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Test Reliability
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Playwright tests for flaky selectors, weak waits, missing assertions, and poor isolation. Suggest concrete fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Page Objects
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a page object plan for these Playwright flows. Include locators, methods, navigation, and expected assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Build Advanced Test Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced suite architecture and CI readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Playwright strategy for authentication state, network mocking, accessibility checks, trace collection, and cross-browser CI.

output_examples.input: A login test fails intermittently after clicking the submit button.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies likely wait and selector issues, recommends role-based locators, and suggests asserting the dashboard URL or heading.

output_examples.input: A team wants to avoid repeated UI login in every test.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a setup project that creates protected storage state and explains how dependent tests can reuse it.

output_examples.input: An API-backed page needs stable tests during backend outages.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes route-based network mocks for success, error, slow response, and request failure scenarios.

best_practices: Prefer role and label locators before CSS selectors or test IDs.
Assert visible user outcomes instead of waiting for generic network idle states.
Keep authentication state and generated artifacts out of shared source control when they contain session data.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on fixed timeouts as the main synchronization strategy.
Do not save real user cookies or tokens in reusable test fixtures.
Do not run the setup checker in untrusted repositories without reviewing local package scripts and binaries.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps write, review, and debug Playwright tests for browser automation, fixtures, selectors, and test configuration.

faq.question: Can it run Playwright tests automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and a setup checker, but command execution depends on the host tool permissions and project environment.

faq.question: Is the included shell script safe?
faq.answer: The script checks local Playwright setup. It uses shell commands, so users should run it only in trusted project directories.

faq.question: Does it support accessibility testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for accessibility audits with Playwright and axe-based testing patterns.

faq.question: Does it support network mocking?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Playwright route handling for mocked responses, failures, slow APIs, and request changes.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
